text: We start with a robotic takedown of the IMA followed by a mini-left thoracotomy for the LED anastomosis.
We start by placing the robotic troll cars, one superiorly and another one inferiorly.
For our instruments, we use a spatula cautery tip with a micro bipolar forceps to take down the IMA as a pedicle.
Once the pedicle is established, the branches are taken down using the micro bipolar forceps.
This is a very safe approach for controlling the branches and also does save time by not exchanging the robotic arms for clip introduction.
The robot also gives you a great visualization all the way up to the subclavian artery as we will see here shortly.
Once you are satisfied with the pedicle, heparin is given and the pericardium is opened widely all the way from the apex of the heart to the brunt ventricular outflow tract.
Once that is accomplished, we start our first method of identifying the LED, which is robotic inspection.
So here in this image, we clearly can identify the apical LED.
We also inspect medially to make sure there are no other vessels that can be visualized and only RV muscle can be seen.
Once that is performed, heparin has taken effect and the IMA pedicle is clipped using the robotic arm twice distally and once proximally.
And then the IMA is cut using endoscopic scissors.
The IMA pedicle is tucked away to the apex of the lung so it doesn't get injured with the thoracotomy incision.
A lung needle is utilized to identify the best intercostal space that would put you on top of the LED exactly by looking at the LED and the needle and deflating the CO2 insufflation.
Once the best in space is established, the second method of identifying the LED starts, which is direct inspection.
As you can imagine, this is very limited as the incision is very small, especially if rib spreading is not utilized.
We don't depend on this method very much.
However, it is reassuring to see the LED exactly through your incision and then you can move on to the next method which involves utilizing TEE.
After that's performed, the stabilizer is placed and the anastomosis can directly be performed in an off-pump manner.
So utilizing these three methods, robotic inspection, direct inspection, and TEE, we are able to identify the LED in almost every case.
And it's important in cases like this, where there are multiple diagonal branches running next to the LED, which can easily confuse the surgeon as to which exactly is the LED.
